examples.title: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Priority Assignment
examples.description: Demonstrates how priorities are assigned based on task periods
examples.explanation: RMS assigns fixed priorities based on task periods. Task with shortest period gets highest priority. This ensures frequently occurring tasks get CPU first.

examples.title: Earliest Deadline First (EDF) Scheduling
examples.description: Dynamic scheduling where task with earliest deadline is executed first
examples.code:
// EARLIEST DEADLINE FIRST (EDF) SCHEDULER

SET currentTime = 0
SET readyQueue = EMPTY

FUNCTION schedule(tasks, currentTime)
BEGIN
  // Add all tasks with deadline >= currentTime to ready queue
  FOR EACH task IN tasks DO
    IF task.deadline >= currentTime THEN
      ADD task TO readyQueue
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // Sort by deadline (earliest first)
  SORT readyQueue BY deadline ASCENDING
  
  // Select task with earliest deadline
  IF readyQueue IS NOT EMPTY THEN
    selectedTask = readyQueue[0]
    RETURN selectedTask
  ELSE
    RETURN IDLE
  END IF
END FUNCTION

// Main scheduler loop
WHILE systemRunning DO
  selected = schedule(availableTasks, currentTime)
  
  IF selected != IDLE THEN
    EXECUTE selected FOR timeSlice
    currentTime = currentTime + timeSlice
    selected.remainingTime = selected.remainingTime - timeSlice
    
    IF selected.remainingTime <= 0 THEN
      REMOVE selected FROM availableTasks
    END IF
  ELSE
    IDLE_PROCESSOR()
  END IF
END WHILE
examples.explanation: EDF is a dynamic scheduling algorithm that recalculates priorities at each scheduling decision based on the earliest deadline. It's optimal for uniprocessor real-time systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) per scheduling decision

examples.title: Priority Inheritance Implementation
examples.description: C code demonstrating priority inheritance to prevent priority inversion
examples.explanation: This demonstrates priority inheritance protocol where a lower priority task holding a lock needed by a higher priority task temporarily gets its priority boosted. This prevents medium priority tasks from preempting the blocked high-priority task.

examples.code:
# Real-Time Task Scheduler Simulation

class RealtimeTask:
    def __init__(self, name, period, execution_time, deadline=None):
        self.name = name
        self.period = period
        self.execution_time = execution_time
        self.deadline = deadline if deadline else period
        self.priority = None
        self.remaining_time = execution_time
        self.next_release = 0
        
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"{self.name}(P:{self.period}, E:{self.execution_time}, D:{self.deadline})"

def rate_monotonic_schedule(tasks, time_horizon):
    """
    Schedule tasks using Rate Monotonic Scheduling
    Shorter period = Higher priority (lower number)
    """
    # Assign priorities based on period (shorter = higher priority)
    sorted_tasks = sorted(tasks, key=lambda t: t.period)
    for i, task in enumerate(sorted_tasks):
        task.priority = i + 1
    
    schedule = []
    current_time = 0
    ready_queue = []
    
    while current_time < time_horizon:
        # Add newly released tasks to ready queue
        for task in sorted_tasks:
            if current_time >= task.next_release:
                if task not in ready_queue:
                    ready_queue.append(task)
                task.next_release += task.period
                task.remaining_time = task.execution_time
        
        # Sort by priority (highest priority first)
        ready_queue.sort(key=lambda t: t.priority)
        
        # Execute highest priority task
        if ready_queue:
            task = ready_queue[0]
            time_slice = min(1, task.remaining_time)  # Execute for 1 time unit
            schedule.append((current_time, task.name, time_slice))
            task.remaining_time -= time_slice
            
            if task.remaining_time <= 0:
                ready_queue.remove(task)
        else:
            schedule.append((current_time, "IDLE", 1))
        
        current_time += 1
    
    return schedule

# Example: Three periodic tasks
tasks = [
    RealtimeTask("Control", period=5, execution_time=2),
    RealtimeTask("Sensor", period=10, execution_time=3),
    RealtimeTask("Display", period=20, execution_time=5)
]

print("Rate Monotonic Scheduling Simulation")
print("Tasks:", tasks)
print("\nSchedule (first 25 time units):")
schedule = rate_monotonic_schedule(tasks, 25)

for time, task_name, duration in schedule:
    print(f"Time {time:2d}: {task_name}")
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates Rate Monotonic Scheduling for real-time tasks. Tasks with shorter periods receive higher priorities. The simulation shows how the scheduler executes tasks in priority order within the time horizon.
